title: Oxygen therapy, CPAP, and neonatal respiratory surveillance
executiveSummary: Administer titrated oxygen therapy, nasal CPAP, and continuous respiratory surveillance in newborns weighing 500 g to 2500 g (days 0–30) with SpO₂ targets adjusted by postnatal age, prevention of oxygen-induced lung injury/retinopathy, and strict logging of FiO₂.
body:
## Steps to perform

1. Evaluate work of breathing (retractions, grunting, nasal flaring, recessions, apnea); monitor HR, RR, preductal SpO₂ (right hand), and postductal SpO₂ if indicated; maintain axillary temperature at 36.5–37.5 °C.
2. Select the device according to severity: low-flow nasal cannulae (LFNC), nasal CPAP, NIV, or mechanical ventilation as prescribed by the neonatologist.
3. Initiate titrated oxygen therapy: use the minimum FiO₂ required to achieve target SpO₂ based on postnatal age and GA (see table); prefer blended room air + oxygen, avoiding sustained pure oxygen.
4. Place nasal CPAP: select a prong/cannula appropriate for weight; set initial pressure at 5–6 cmH₂O (adjust between 4–8 cmH₂O based on response); secure without excessive pressure on the nose.
5. Verify the circuit: active humidification at 37 °C, pressure measured at the Y-piece, absence of massive leaks; ensure pressure and FiO₂ alarms are active.
6. Record every shift and after any changes: FiO₂, PEEP/CPAP, RR, SpO₂, pH/PaCO₂ if blood gas analysis is performed, and clinical findings.
7. Position the patient in alternating prone or lateral recumbency according to protocol; elevate the head of the bed slightly if tolerated; avoid cervical flexion that may obstruct the airway.
8. Aspirate secretions only if they obstruct the airway; use an aseptic technique with suction pressure ≤ 80–100 mmHg in newborns < 1500 g.
9. Monitor for signs of CPAP overload: subcutaneous emphysema, pneumothorax, abdominal distension, nasal erythema; reduce pressure or escalate according to prescription.
10. Weaning: reduce FiO₂ before weaning CPAP; criteria for discontinuation are based on clinical stability, SpO₂ in room air, and the absence of significant apneas.
11. In newborns < 32 weeks or < 1500 g: perform retinopathy screening and follow-up according to protocol; limit SpO₂ to 91–95% in preterm infants (avoid sustained > 95%).
12. In case of sudden deterioration (SpO₂ drop, cyanosis, apnea): temporarily increase FiO₂, ventilate with bag and mask if apnea occurs, notify immediately, and prepare for intubation.

## Scientific rationale

### Population and scope

**Newborns day 0–30**, **500 g–2500 g**. Neonatal oxygen therapy requires strict titration; both hypoxemia and hyperoxia (especially in preterm infants) cause morbidity (oxygen-induced lung/retina injury, ROP, lung injury).

### Indications

- Tachypnea, retractions, or expiratory grunting.
- SpO₂ below the target range for postnatal age/GA.
- Apnea of prematurity or related bradycardia.
- Respiratory distress syndrome (RDS), TTN, or neonatal pneumonia.
- Post-extubation or weaning from mechanical ventilation.

### Evidence and recommendations

> **Source:** VON / Neonatal Oxygenation Prospective Meta-analysis — In preterm infants < 28 weeks, a target SpO₂ of 91–95% reduces mortality compared to wider ranges; avoid sustained > 95% (Vermont Oxford Network, 2023).

> **Source:** AAP/NRP — Titrate FiO₂ from birth; follow preductal targets per minute of life during resuscitation (American Academy of Pediatrics, 2021).

### Target SpO₂ according to gestational age

| Group | Target SpO₂ |
| --- | --- |
| ≥ 35 weeks (term/near-term) | 92–97% |
| 28–34 weeks | 91–95% |
| < 28 weeks / < 1000 g | 91–95% (strict) |

Adjust according to neonatologist prescription and blood gas values (PaO₂ 50–80 mmHg as a rough guide in preterm infants).

### Respiratory support selection

| Level | Indication | Initial parameters |
| --- | --- | --- |
| LFNC | Mild hypoxemia, post-CPAP | 0.5–2 L/min (actual flow limited in small newborns) |
| Nasal CPAP | Mild-to-moderate RDS, TTN, post-extubation | 5–6 cmH₂O; titrated FiO₂ |
| NIV (NIPPV) | Persistent apnea, atelectasis | CPAP + pressure support according to ventilator settings |
| Invasive MV | CPAP failure, PaCO₂ > 60 with acidosis | According to neonatal ventilation protocol |

### CPAP interface care

| Weight | Guideline prong size |
| --- | --- |
| 500–1000 g | Small preterm |
| 1000–1500 g | Medium preterm |
| 1500–2500 g | Large preterm / term |

Rotate prongs every 6–12 hours; protect nasal skin with a hydrocolloid dressing. Humidify to 100%; gas temperature at 37 °C.

### Continuous respiratory surveillance

- **Alarms:** Bradycardia < 80 bpm, apnea > 20 s, SpO₂ < target − 5% for > 30 s.
- Record apnea/bradycardia events on the monitor logs.
- Perform blood gas analysis if FiO₂ > 40%, CPAP > 7 cmH₂O, or if clinical deterioration occurs.

### Complications — notify immediately

- Pneumothorax (thoracic asymmetry, decreased breath sounds, tracheal deviation).
- Cervical/facial subcutaneous emphysema.
- Oxygen-induced lung injury in preterm infants (saturations > 95% with low FiO₂).
- Frequent apneas accompanied by bradycardia (< 60 bpm) or cyanosis.
- Severe nasal injury or septal perforation due to poorly secured CPAP.

bibliography.citation: Vermont Oxford Network. Oxygen targeting and respiratory care in the NICU.
American Academy of Pediatrics. Neonatal Resuscitation Program — oxygen titration and CPAP.
World Health Organization. WHO recommendations on newborn health — thermal care and respiratory support.
Askie LM, et al. Neonatal Oxygenation Prospective Meta-analysis (NeOProM) — target ranges for SpO2. Cochrane and related trials.
SATI — Consensus on neonatal ventilation and respiratory support.
